content.assessment: Delaying selection to 14-16 directly targets the cohort-and-geography interaction: fewer sorting points during a period of demographic contraction could reduce the number of institutions competing for a shrinking pool of pre-adolescents, potentially easing the closure pressure on the 270 endangered schools by keeping cohorts pooled longer. However, the reform's viability depends on a stable planning horizon and workforce capacity that current demographic and staffing trends (aging teacher workforce) call into question.
opportunities: Shrinking cohorts create administrative opportunity to consolidate/restructure school networks around later selection without needing new infrastructure, since declining rolls already necessitate reorganization
strengths: Reduces the number of sorting points precisely when cohort volume is falling, potentially concentrating scarce pupils in fewer, more viable comprehensive units rather than fragmenting them across selective and non-selective tracks
Cross-national precedent (Poland) demonstrates that delaying selection can coexist with an improving demographic/labor-market trajectory
threats: High symbolic salience and opposition mobilization capacity could block reform before its cohort-stabilizing benefits materialize
If declining cohorts are used as a rationale for closures under the current structure before this reform can be enacted, the window for reorganizing around comprehensive middle schools may close
weaknesses: Reform reversal risk (Poland post-2016) suggests uncertain durability over the multi-decade planning horizon needed to see cohort-level effects mature

Aging teacher workforce (half 50+, only 7.2% under 30) raises capacity concerns for implementing a structurally different comprehensive system precisely as cohort numbers used to justify restructuring are shrinking
